results.title: [Increase of prilocaine-induced methemoglobinemia following anesthesia induction].
results.abstract: Although the local anaesthetic prilocaine is less cardio- and neurotoxic than lidocaine, it bears the disadvantage of the formation of methaemoglobin by the metabolite o-toluidine. Prilocaine is often successfully used, especially for the blockade of the brachial plexus, but one problem of this technique is the failure rate of 3-10%, with the consequence that general anaesthesia after administration of prilocaine is frequently necessary. Methaemoglobin formation after prilocaine administration has been thoroughly investigated. Nothing is known, however, about the interactions of prilocaine and the induction of general anaesthesia relative to methaemoglobinaemia. CASE REPORT. Two patients (47 and 52 years old) each received 500 mg prilocaine for the axillary blockade of the brachial plexus. After 100 and 120 min respectively, it was necessary to induce general anaesthesia, for which 350 mg thiopental, 1 mg alfentanil and 45 mg atracurium were used.
